element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player controls Dallas against CPU Chicago in opening match
Integrated into all matches; necessary for team coordination
Defensive positioning becomes critical against stronger teams
Essential vs teams with faster throws
Evasion option; becomes critical against power shots
Increases throw damage; essential for efficient eliminations

element_introduction_order.first_appears: First match; only accessible to team captain
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Faster, higher-damage throw; critical against late-game opponents

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Second tournament stage (England) when AI team loses members
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Foreign teams introduce 3-4 backup players; increases match complexity

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Integrated into all matches in NES version
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Each team member has unique abilities; affects strategy
Sequential matches vs: England → Iceland → China → Africa/Kenya → Japan
Team with most survivors wins if time expires; affects late-match tactics

typical_tension_triggers: Time running out with close survivor count
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Cyclical exchange rhythm: offense-defense cycles within each match, with escalating opponent difficulty across tournament progression. Early matches allow learning; mid-tournament matches introduce backup substitutes and stat variations; late-tournament matches (Japan) present peak difficulty. Time limit creates late-match tension spike.

difficulty_curve.early_game: Dallas vs Chicago opening match establishes mechanics; Dallas is player's team (standard difficulty). Learning curve for throw/catch/pass mechanics.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Tournament stages 2-5 (England, Iceland, China, Africa/Kenya): Foreign teams introduce defensive backup players (3-4 extras per team). Individual player stat variations become strategically important in NES version.
difficulty_curve.late_game: Japan final match represents peak difficulty. After victory, tournament cycles but repeats from England with potentially escalated difficulty on subsequent cycles (not documented in sources).
notable_spikes: First international opponent (England): introduces foreign team mechanics with backup players
Japan match: final opponent featuring maximum team depth and captain capability

notable_level_patterns.description: Linear progression through 7 international opponents (8 matches including opening Dallas/Chicago). Each defeated team triggers replacement cycle starting from England if tournament continues. Sequential stage: Dallas/Chicago → England → Iceland → China → Africa/Kenya → Japan → (cycle repeats).

Early matches (Dallas/Chicago) feature single team interaction. Mid-tournament matches introduce backup player substitution system where defeated foreign team members are replaced from bench (visible in later stages). Late-tournament (Japan) features full team depth.

NES version uniquely features individual stats per player and country-specific stage music (e.g., Japan stage: 'Sakura Sakura', England stage: Beatles-mashup medley). Japanese version features Kunio-kun series continuity; US version reframed as US team competing internationally.
Separate competitive mode supporting 2-player (NES) or 4-player (Japanese Famicom with Joy Pair peripheral) simultaneous play. Alternates with World Cup campaign.

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Super Dodge Ball NES)
